flashcards.back: A graph traversal algorithm that explores all neighbors of the current vertex before moving to the next level. It uses a queue and a visited array. It visits vertices in order of their distance from the source.
Time: O(V + E) with adjacency list, O(V^2) with adjacency matrix. Space: O(V) for the queue and visited array.

flashcards.front: Why does BFS find shortest paths in unweighted graphs?
flashcards.back: BFS visits vertices level by level (distance 0, then 1, then 2, ...). The first time a vertex is reached, it is via the shortest path. No shorter path can be found later because all shorter paths have already been explored.
BFS(source): mark source visited, enqueue source. While queue not empty: dequeue vertex v, for each unvisited neighbor u of v: mark u visited, enqueue u.

flashcards.front: What is a BFS spanning tree?
flashcards.back: The tree formed by the edges used to discover new vertices during BFS. It is a shortest-path tree: the path from root to any vertex in this tree has the minimum number of edges.

flashcards.front: What are the applications of BFS?
flashcards.back: 1) Shortest path in unweighted graphs, 2) Level-order traversal, 3) Finding connected components, 4) Bipartite graph testing, 5) Web crawling, 6) Social network friend suggestions.
